about.title: About
paragraphs: I am a 25-year-old from Oulu with a strong passion for programming. My social skills and ability to listen help me understand people's needs and turn them into working digital solutions.

I am a diligent builder who sees projects through to completion. I am self-taught and have worked with React, React Native, JavaScript, HTML, CSS, and Java — with React and React Native at the center of my focus.
In my free time I listen to music, play games, and create my own music. Below is one of my tracks on SoundCloud.

resume.title: Résumé
resume.summary: 25 years old, originally from Kemijärvi, military service completed. Business graduate by education. Strengths include self-directed learning, diligence, and teamwork. Interested in game and website development.

notes.title: Notes
notes.description: Short write-ups on game design, web development, and lessons from active projects.

portfolio-next16-rebuild.title: Rebuilding the portfolio on Next.js 16
portfolio-next16-rebuild.excerpt: Why I moved from a static site to App Router, next-intl, and a data-driven project model.
body: The old portfolio worked, but every new project meant touching layout code or duplicating HTML patterns. I wanted a system where structure stays stable and content moves through typed data and translation files.

Next.js 16 with the App Router gave me static project and note routes, server components for SEO, and a contact API without bolting on a separate backend. next-intl keeps Finnish as the default while English stays first-class — same URLs, same components, different message files.

Projects now split into base data (images, slugs, categories) and locale copy (titles, case studies, metadata). That separation makes it easy to add a project once and ship it in both languages on the next deploy.

neon-drift-survival-loop.title: Designing Neon Drift's survival loop
neon-drift-survival-loop.excerpt: How upgrade synergies and pressure from all directions shape a browser arcade that rewards mastery.
body: Neon Drift started as a hidden experiment inside the portfolio and grew into a standalone survival shooter. The core loop is simple: survive, collect upgrades, stack synergies, die, try again — but the threat model comes from every direction at once, so positioning matters as much as reflexes.

Canvas 2D keeps the frame budget predictable on mid-range laptops and phones. Upgrades are designed as readable combinations rather than opaque stat bumps — players should feel why a build works after one or two runs.
The global leaderboard adds long-term motivation without forcing competitive mechanics into the first minute. Auth and Postgres came later; the loop had to be fun in a local session first.

narrative-puzzles-on-the-web.title: Narrative puzzles on the web
narrative-puzzles-on-the-web.excerpt: Two early prototypes — Nothing Pending and The Archive Incident — and what Papers, Please taught me about pressure.
body: Nothing Pending borrows the document-review tension from Papers, Please: short decisions, visible consequences, and a system that looks bureaucratic until it isn't. The goal is not to clone the genre but to learn how pressure creates story without cutscenes.

The Archive Incident takes a different angle: semi-hard puzzles inside a digital archive, rendered with PixiJS for responsive scene layers while Next.js handles routing, content, and future auth. Clues are meant to be pieced together across screens, not solved in isolation.
Both projects are intentionally early. I am validating feel and readability before investing in full content pipelines — the same discipline I apply to arcade loops in Neon Drift.

neon-drift.title: Neon Drift
neon-drift.subtitle: Browser survival arcade
neon-drift.description: Neon Drift is a standalone browser survival shooter: dodge swarms from every direction, stack upgrades and synergies, and push for a spot on the global board. It started as a hidden portfolio experiment and now runs as its own app.

caseStudy.title: Case study — Neon Drift
caseStudy.role: Solo developer — game design, Canvas 2D implementation, Next.js app shell, leaderboard integration
caseStudy.problem: I needed a portfolio piece that proved I could ship a complete interactive product, not just UI demos. The game had to run in the browser without installs, feel good on keyboard and touch, and leave room for a real progression hook.
caseStudy.approach: I built a tight survival loop on Canvas 2D with readable upgrade synergies and threats from all directions. The app shell uses Next.js for routing and deployment; Neon Postgres and auth arrived when a global leaderboard became worth the operational cost.
caseStudy.outcome: Neon Drift graduated from a hidden experiment into a standalone playable product with its own deploy target and optional global scores. It demonstrates end-to-end delivery: feel, performance, persistence, and production hosting.
learnings: Validate the core loop locally before adding auth or database complexity.
Synergy readability matters more than raw upgrade count in replayable arcades.
Canvas 2D is still a strong choice when draw calls and particle counts are predictable.

eternal-cry.title: Eternal-Cry
eternal-cry.subtitle: Video game
eternal-cry.description: An action platformer with a rich story, action, secrets, and enemies. Built with GameMaker Studio. I also started a website for the project. Currently on hold.

caseStudy.title: Case study — Eternal-Cry
caseStudy.role: Solo developer — worldbuilding, level design, GameMaker Studio implementation, promotional site draft
caseStudy.problem: I wanted to build a narrative action platformer with exploration and secrets, but scope quickly outpaced solo capacity. Without a clear vertical slice, the project risked becoming an endless design document.
caseStudy.approach: I prototyped combat, platforming, and enemy encounters in GameMaker Studio while drafting a companion site for lore and updates. Progress was measured in playable rooms rather than feature lists, and the project was paused when other commitments required focus.
caseStudy.outcome: Eternal-Cry produced a tangible vertical slice and art direction, but remains on hold. It still informs how I scope game projects today: story beats follow playable proof, not the other way around.
learnings: Ambitious narrative games need a ruthless vertical slice before world scale expands.
Tooling familiarity (GameMaker) accelerated iteration more than engine novelty.
Pausing a project with documented progress is better than shipping an incoherent half-game.

nothing-pending.title: Nothing Pending
nothing-pending.subtitle: Next.js — early development
nothing-pending.description: A Next.js project inspired by Papers, Please. Players review documents and make judgment calls under pressure. Super early in development.

caseStudy.title: Case study — Nothing Pending
caseStudy.role: Solo developer — systems design, UI flow, Next.js prototype
caseStudy.problem: I wanted to learn how document-review tension translates to the web — short decisions, moral weight, and rules that look neutral but carry consequences. The challenge is conveying pressure without a dedicated game engine pipeline.
caseStudy.approach: The prototype focuses on a single review desk flow: inspect documents, cross-check rules, approve or deny under time pressure. Next.js handles routing and stateful UI; content is structured so new rule sets can be added without rewriting the shell.
caseStudy.outcome: Nothing Pending is in super early development with a playable desk loop and readable rule feedback. It validates that the Papers, Please formula can live inside a web app before investing in full narrative content.
learnings: Bureaucratic UI is a feature — clarity and friction can coexist.
Rule exceptions are cheaper to test as data than as hard-coded branches.
Early prototypes should answer one question: does the decision feel consequential?

the-archive-incident.title: The Archive Incident
the-archive-incident.subtitle: Next.js + PixiJS — early development
the-archive-incident.description: A semi-hard puzzle experience built with Next.js and PixiJS. Players piece together clues inside a digital archive. Early in development.

caseStudy.title: Case study — The Archive Incident
caseStudy.role: Solo developer — puzzle design, PixiJS scene layers, Next.js app integration
caseStudy.problem: I needed a puzzle experience that felt spatial and tactile on the web, not just a form with riddles. Clues had to connect across screens while difficulty stayed semi-hard — challenging without opaque logic leaps.
caseStudy.approach: PixiJS renders the archive scenes and interactive hotspots; Next.js owns navigation, content, and future save states. Puzzles are authored as linked clue chains so players assemble understanding over multiple views rather than one modal answer.
caseStudy.outcome: The Archive Incident is in early development with core scene navigation and initial puzzle chains playable. It proves the split stack: PixiJS for presence, Next.js for structure.
learnings: Semi-hard puzzles need fair foreshadowing — every clue should point forward and backward.
Splitting render (PixiJS) from app shell (Next.js) keeps iteration fast on both sides.
Archive fantasy sells mystery best when the UI looks mundane and the gaps feel intentional.
